answers.field_label: Why Do You Want To Volunteer At Carewest
answers.value:
I want to volunteer at Carewest because I’ve seen first-hand how much of a difference consistent, compassionate support can make for people who are aging, in rehabilitation, or living with chronic conditions. My grandmother lived in a long-term care facility in Vancouver for the last six years of her life, and the volunteers there became a real source of joy for her—whether it was someone who came by just to play cards with her once a week or a volunteer who helped organize a small birthday celebration when our family couldn’t all travel to see her. Those experiences really stayed with me and shaped how I think about quality of life in continuing care.

I recently moved to Calgary for work and, after getting settled, I’ve been looking for a meaningful way to give back locally that isn’t just a one-time event but something ongoing and relationship-based. Carewest stood out to me because of its strong reputation in the community, the range of programs it offers, and the focus on person-centred care. I spent some time looking through the Carewest website and reading about the different sites and programs, and I was especially drawn to the idea of one-to-one visiting, leisure activities, and community outings. I like the idea of building a regular connection with residents, learning their stories, and being a small but reliable part of their weekly routine.

Professionally, I work remotely as a project coordinator for a software company, which means I spend a lot of time in front of a screen. Volunteering with Carewest would balance that by letting me be physically present with people, using my interpersonal skills instead of just my computer skills. I’m comfortable talking with people from different backgrounds, listening to them, and adapting to their communication style—skills I’ve developed both through my job and through previous volunteer work with seniors at a community centre in Burnaby.

I’m also very interested in learning more about the health-care environment in Alberta. While I’m not a nurse or doctor, I’ve always been fascinated by how multi-disciplinary teams work together in care settings. Volunteering at Carewest feels like a way to contribute in a practical, down-to-earth way, while also gaining a deeper appreciation for the challenges and rewards of continuing care.

On a personal level, I find it very grounding to spend time with older adults and people in recovery. They often have a different perspective on what matters, and conversations with them tend to be more reflective and less rushed than what I’m used to in the tech world. I value that. I’d like to be someone residents can count on to show up when I say I will, to remember the little details about their lives—like how they take their tea, what sports team they follow, or which music they enjoy—and to bring a calm, friendly presence.

I also appreciate that Carewest offers structured volunteer roles, orientation, and training. I don’t want to just “drop in and help”; I want to learn how to be truly useful and respectful in a care environment, whether that’s understanding infection prevention practices, respecting residents’ privacy and autonomy, or knowing how to support someone who might be having a difficult day emotionally or cognitively.

In short, I want to volunteer at Carewest because I’m looking for a long-term, meaningful way to contribute to my new community, to support residents’ quality of life in very practical ways, and to honour the kind of kindness that volunteers once showed to my own family.

answers.value:
I have a number of hobbies and interests that I think could be useful and enjoyable in a volunteer setting at Carewest:

1. Reading and book discussions: I’m an avid reader—mostly contemporary fiction, memoirs, and some historical non-fiction. I enjoy reading aloud and have experience doing so from volunteering at a seniors’ centre in Burnaby, where I used to read short stories, news articles, and poetry to a small group once a week. I’d be happy to read to residents who have visual impairments or simply enjoy listening, and to facilitate informal discussions about books or stories.

2. Music: I don’t play an instrument professionally, but I play basic guitar and know a lot of classic songs from the 50s through the 80s (Beatles, Elvis, Motown, folk songs, etc.). I’ve previously helped lead small sing-along sessions using lyric sheets, which residents really enjoyed. I’m also comfortable using a Bluetooth speaker to play playlists tailored to residents’ preferences—oldies, jazz, big band, or soft rock—and adjusting volume and song choices based on the mood and setting.

3. Arts and crafts: I enjoy simple crafting activities like card-making, colouring, collage, and basic watercolour painting. I’m not an artist, but I’m patient and good at breaking down activities into manageable steps. In my past volunteer role, I helped residents make seasonal decorations and birthday cards for family members. I’m comfortable helping people with fine-motor challenges by pre-cutting materials, offering hand-over-hand support when appropriate, and making sure the activity is more about enjoyment than about “perfect” results.

4. Walking and light exercise: I like taking walks myself and generally try to stay active. I’m comfortable walking slowly with someone using a walker or wheelchair (as long as I get proper training on safety and protocols). I enjoy supporting gentle movement, like accompanying residents on short walks in hallways or gardens when appropriate, and encouraging safe participation in chair exercises led by staff.

5. Conversation and reminiscence: I genuinely enjoy listening to people’s life stories—where they grew up, what work they did, their families, and their hobbies. I’ve found that many older adults like to talk about “how things used to be,” and I’m happy to ask open-ended questions and follow their lead. I’m patient with repetition and comfortable with residents who may have memory loss or dementia, understanding that the goal is to make them feel heard and respected in the moment.

6. Technology basics: Because I work in tech, I’m fairly comfortable with tablets, smartphones, and video calls. If appropriate and within Carewest guidelines, I’d be happy to support residents with simple tasks like helping them start a video call with family, scroll through family photos, or listen to a favourite song on YouTube or Spotify. I understand there are privacy and consent considerations and would always follow staff direction.

7. Baking and food-related activities: While I know volunteers can’t always cook directly with residents, I enjoy baking at home and have experience helping with food-related social activities (like decorating cookies, plating snacks, or serving tea and coffee at events). I’m comfortable following food safety instructions, wearing gloves, and being mindful of dietary restrictions and allergies.

8. Languages and culture: I speak English fluently and conversational Vietnamese (my parents are from Vietnam). I’m always curious about different cultures and traditions and enjoy learning about other people’s backgrounds. I think this helps me connect with residents from diverse communities and be respectful of different customs, holidays, and preferences.

Overall, my hobbies and interests are fairly simple and people-oriented. I like any activity that gives people a chance to connect, express themselves, and feel like their day had something a little special in it—even if it’s as small as listening to a favourite song, sharing a story, or playing a short game of cards.

answers.value: I am available to volunteer 1.5 to 3 hours per shift, ideally 1–2 times per week. My preferred schedule would be one weekday morning (for example, Monday or Friday from about 9:30 a.m. to 12:00 p.m.) and/or one weekday afternoon (such as Tuesday from 1:00 p.m. to 3:30 p.m.). Because I work remotely with some flexibility in my hours, I can adjust slightly within those windows to match program needs, as long as we set a consistent weekly schedule. I am looking for a longer-term commitment of at least 6–12 months, rather than a short-term placement, so that I can build stable relationships with residents and the care team.
